author: @christopherburman3340
text: Interesting Tsunami has rice husks. I want to try the sheoak (casaurina) brew from another episode but it seems the sheoak does not contain much Si so I am planning to add rice husks. Any idea if that would lift the Si in the final brew?

author: @NutriTechSolutionsNTS
text:
Thanks for the question! Tsunami is based on rice husks, though the active component is relatively complex extraction. 

Yes, She oak (Casuarina) has been a common ingredient for BD508 in Australia, though there isn't a lot of research on it's actual silica content. Rice husks have been studied more widely and are a very good source of bioavailable silica, with it making up about 15–20% of the husk dry weight. So including it in a well managed brew would certainly deliver more available Silica.

There are other pH factors to be mindful of but ashes from these plant materials can be another highly concentrated source of Silica, for reference Rice Husk ashes can range from 74–98% SiO₂.

author: @christopherburman3340
text: ​@NutriTechSolutionsNTS I asked AI for info on rice husks and yes it seems making the Si is a headache for DIY people. And I suspect that the process of making AI in rice husks plant available will increase the pH which I want to avoid because it then becomes incompatible with most other fertilisers

author: @NutriTechSolutionsNTS
text:
Thanks for the comment! They all help with the algorithm to further highlight our videos featuring "Bug in a Jug".

I suggest you look deeper into the different classes of microbes in relation to their association with plants and other microbes. Compost is an incredible tool, but it still has many limitations when it comes to culturing the complete range of beneficial microbes. 

Obligate Symbionts (Plant-Reliant Microbes)The microbes that form the most intimate, beneficial plant partnerships generally do not survive standard composting. 

Arbuscular Mycorrhizal Fungi (AMF): They are obligate symbionts, meaning they must be connected to a living root to reproduce and thrive. The high temperatures of the active composting phase will typically destroy their delicate spores and hyphae.

Symbiotic Nitrogen-Fixing Bacteria (Rhizobium): While they can survive in soil, they are highly specific. For effective inoculation, you must use specialized, host-specific inoculants (e.g., coating legume seeds) rather than relying on a general compost mix.

author: @howardroe7515
text: Is there much difference between EM and BAM?

author: @johnharris7244
text: Great question,  I brewed up some EM1 a couple of weeks ago and it has very distinctive sweet aroma , yesterday I opened up a container of BAM and it smells very similar and that was my exact thought.

author: @NutriTechSolutionsNTS
text:
Thanks for the question @howardroe7515!

Yes, there are some differences between EM (Effective Microorganisms) and BAM (Beneficial Anaerobic Microbes), although they share similar foundational concepts. Both products are designed to enhance soil health and plant growth through the use of beneficial microorganisms, but they have distinct compositions and applications:

EM (Effective Microorganisms): Developed by Professor Teruo Higa, EM is a blend of various beneficial microorganisms, including lactic acid bacteria, yeast, and photosynthetic bacteria. It is widely used in agriculture to improve soil health, composting, and plant growth.

BAM (Beneficial Anaerobic Microbes): BAM is based on the original EM formula but includes additional microbial varieties. It contains over 80 different microbe varieties, including lactobacillus, purple non-sulphur bacteria (PNSB), fermenting fungi, yeast, and actinomycetes. BAM is designed to perform similar functions to EM but with a broader spectrum of microorganisms.

author: @johnharris7244
text: ​@NutriTechSolutionsNTSdoes that make BAM a good foliage spray option.

author: @NutriTechSolutionsNTS
text: Yes, BAM is a great all rounder, it's certainly helpful via foliar, though it's most effective in the soil, especially for recovering anaerobic soils.

author: @davidspghill
text: Question about the foliar calcium, if we use chelated calcium nitrate, on the one hand, good for foliar calcium but I'm trying to avoid nitrate applications for the reason mentioned in the foliar urea section of the vid.  Do you have any idea how impactful foliar calnitrate is upon plant energy in this context, assuming here the plant absorbs it in leaves and then needs to convert it to amino which will take lots of energy. Any alternatives to foliar chelated calcium nitrate?

author: @NutriTechSolutionsNTS
text:
Thanks for the great question @davidspghill!

This really depends on the required outcome. If this specifically to deal with a calcium deficiency, a chelated form of Calcium Nitrate is still one of the most practical methods we would recommend, due to it's high availability, solubility and ease of application. An average crop requires 10-12 times more nitrogen than calcium, so this is still just a fraction of the overall N required. The remaining N could be applied via foliar urea applications throughout the season.

Otherwise you could opt for micronised lime, it has a much lower effective foliar uptake rate, but due to the lower cost per kilogram it still works out at a similar cost per hectare. It does require slightly more specialised spray equipment due to the insoluble particles and the need for constant agitation during application, but it is certainly another practical option. It would also allow for the inclusion of chelated urea in the same application.

It's true that nitrate requires around 8 times more energy for foliar uptake than urea, but this is just one aspect. If you have a severe calcium deficiency the increased energy production due to it's alleviation will easily counteract the additional energy required to utilise the nitrate.

I hope that helps!

text: Loved this, thanks. So, does the BioBubble mixture replace a traditional sourdough starter? Also, I’m diagnosed coeliac. The lime mint gluten-sensitive version appeals but in bread?  I’d love to be able to make bread my body will not just tolerate but benefit from.

text:
Thanks for the question @PaulandDotFaint-t2g!

Yes, it can be used to replace a Sourdough starter or in addition to, depending on your desired outcome. I'm sorry to hear, if you are truly Coeliac this may not be the recipe for you. Even after 48 hours, a significant proportion of gluten proteins remain intact, so you would have to opt for a completely gluten free sourdough recipe. Yes, the lime mint flavour could be an issue, though at that rate it shouldn't be overly strong. If it's still too overpowering you could brew out the GF BioBubble similar to a sourdough starter, which would allow you to reduce the flavour significantly. 

I would recommend looking into natural healing solutions for Coeliac disease, it generally relates to a poor gut lining, which with the right diet, probiotics and prebiotics can be fixed.

author: @geoffeverist4264
text: Great video, can you use too much of these products ?? , also do we have the same leaching issues as chemical fertilisers have if we use too much ??

author: @NutriTechSolutionsNTS
text:
Thanks for the question @geoffeverist4264!

Yes, it is possible to use too much, though as long as you stick to the recommended rates there shouldn't be any issues. As these recipes are all buffered with humates it significantly reduces this risk, though if you are dealing with particularly sensitive plants it's a smart idea to test this on a small area before applying it widely.

If you compare these foliar rates per hectare to standard fertiliser applications, these are still very small amounts, and again the humates significantly reduce the potential for leaching. So this shouldn't be an issue, but as per standard foliar recommendations, this shouldn't occur during high rainfall events.

I hope that helps!

author: @user-p8o1g
text: Hi. Some really interesting points here. I am a sugarcane farmers in south africa, we harvest our crop 18-24months- when would you apply a urea and triacontonol foliar to assist with " ripening" as such in stead of using chemical ripeners? 8 weeks before harvest or better to apply in the spring/summer before we harvest? This season we have found with the great rains brings lower sugar content I a think applying some nutriets, possibly even Potassium and Tricontonol will be a great in impoving sugar content without needing to use chemical ripeners.

author: @NutriTechSolutionsNTS
text:
Great question! If you're looking to boost sugar content in sugarcane naturally without using chemical ripeners, a foliar spray of triacontanol, low-rate urea, and potassium can work really well—especially in wet seasons where dilution is an issue. 

Best timing: Apply the foliar spray about 6–8 weeks before harvest. This is when the cane is still active but starting to transition into sugar accumulation. Applying too early can promote more stalk growth instead of ripening.

Recommended foliar mix (per hectare):

Triacontanol: 10–20 mL

Urea: 0.5% to 1% (500g–1kg per 100L)

Potassium (like KNO₃ or potassium sulfate): 3–5 kg

Optional: Fulvic acid (250–500 mL) to improve uptake

Use 200–300L water/ha with a good surfactant

If your season has had heavy rains, you could also apply an earlier spray (around 12–14 weeks before harvest at 1/2 rates) to begin shifting the plant from vegetative to ripening mode, then follow up with the main spray at 6–8 weeks out.

text:

However, what I am struggling with is if, for example, the old leaves are deficient in K compared with new leaves, how much K should I add? 

The other thing is the a fairly recent blog on the Soil Doctor website that states he sent batches of leaves from same sample to three labs and received different results from each - so chose yr lab carefully

author: @NutriTechSolutionsNTS
text:
Thanks for the question @christopherburman3340 !

Graeme has written a great two-part article on this. Here are the links below,
https://blog.nutri-tech.com.au/the-k-factor-1/
https://blog.nutri-tech.com.au/the-k-factor-2/

Generally, the recommendations for deficiencies identified via tissue/sap analysis will be foliar applications, with the rates being more related to the uptake capacity of different crops rather than the level of deficiencies. For a more severe deficiency, we would recommend a series of repeat applications and then retesting to confirm the level of improvement.

K-Rich™ is a popular choice for a foliar correction of K shortage. This concentrated formula features fully buffered liquid potassium (35%) that provides a particularly impressive potassium response. Otherwise, a good DIY option is a foliar application of 5 – 10 kg per hectare of potassium sulphate combined with around 200 g of NTS Fulvic Acid Powder™, which can also significantly reduce visible drought stress in drought situations.

author: @philosophiesofspinozanietz294
text: You are questioning the primacy of calcium. You are also reemphasizibg the role of magnesium. You also complicating the picture which I dislike. You also talk too fast please

author: @NutriTechSolutionsNTS
text:
Thanks for your input. But really though? 

If you watched the episode prior to this one, you will hear a whole lot from Graeme on the importance of Calcium. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=lZmRCMYbzik&t=81s

In reality there is no "primacy" of any one mineral or nutrient, they are all dependent upon each other. Yes, we certainly need more Calcium by weight than Magnesium per hectare for a balanced base saturation, but that doesn't change the fact that chlorophyll, the fundamental molecule for photosynthesis is built around Magnesium. These are all important players and they all have their part to play.

I suggest you watch some of the other episodes before jumping to assumptions. Nutrition Farming is a dynamic dance with extremely complex natural systems, it is inherently complicated, there are more simplistic alternatives if that is what you really seek. Also feel free to make use of the built in "Play Back Speed" settings in the YouTube player, if you think the speed is too fast (Bottom right).
